summary: Inc-06A 3-lens adversarial review: code-reviewer + qa-test-engineer + security-architect over the diff, each verdict adversarially self-checked

review.lens: Correctness, exact-process/verbatim-message fidelity, transaction/atomicity, Modulith boundary integrity, C4 read-seam shape, and hand-written mapper field-order correctness for INC-06A (clinical-order top-up, 7 chunks).

findings.title: C7 multipart upload cap is unreachable for files 1 MiB–10 MiB — Spring's default 1 MB resolver limit breaks the 10 MiB legacy-parity cap
findings.detail: The in-code cap (storageProperties.maxFileSizeBytes() = 10485760) is enforced inside LabTestService.uploadAttachment / RadiologyService.uploadAttachment, but there is NO spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size / max-request-size configured anywhere (application.yml ends at hmis.attachments.max-file-size-bytes: 10485760; grep over backend/src/main finds no multipart resolver config). Spring Boot defaults to 1 MB per file / 10 MB per request, so any upload between ~1 MB and 10 MiB — which legacy explicitly ALLOWS (PatientServiceImpl.java:2838-2840 caps at 10485760) — is rejected by the MultipartResolver with MaxUploadSizeExceededException BEFORE reaching the controller, never returning the verbatim 422 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed'. This is a behaviour/parity regression: the effective real-world cap is ~1 MB, not 10 MiB. The AttachmentUploadCapIT passes only because it uses an 11-byte file under a tiny 10-byte test override (both far below Spring's 1 MB default), so the resolver never trips — the test masks the gap rather than covering the 1–10 MiB band.
findings.recommendation: Add spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size at >= 10485760 (e.g. 10MB / 11MB) so the in-code 10 MiB gate is the binding limit and emits the verbatim 422. Add an IT that uploads a fixture above 1 MB but below 10 MiB and asserts 201 (proves the resolver no longer pre-empts the in-code gate), plus one above 10 MiB asserting the verbatim 422.

findings.title: C7 attachment-upload guard ORDER diverges from legacy (size-first vs legacy count→status→size; status-before-count) — golden-master message drift on overlapping-error inputs
findings.detail: Legacy lab upload checks guards in the order: existence(404, PatientResource.java:5950) → count==5 (PatientServiceImpl.java:2828-2830 'Can not add more than 5 attachments') → status!=COLLECTED (2832-2834 'Can only attach for collected tests') → size>cap (2838-2840 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed'); radiology is identical with ACCEPTED (2926-2938). The as-built uploadAttachment checks size FIRST (before existence and before status/count), then resolves the order, then evaluates canAttach() which reports the STATUS message before the COUNT message. Consequences: (a) oversize file on a non-COLLECTED/ACCEPTED order returns 'File exceeds...' in as-built vs 'Can only attach...' in legacy; (b) oversize file on a non-existent order returns 422 'File exceeds...' vs legacy 404; (c) 5 attachments on a non-COLLECTED order returns the status message vs legacy's count message. The javadoc asserting 'Guard order (legacy-parity)' and 'The legacy gate checks status first then count; the same order is preserved here' is factually incorrect — legacy checks count before status and size last.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/LabTestService.java:576-593 (uploadAttachment); backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/RadiologyService.java:1076-1093; domain canAttach() at LabTest.java:661-663 and Radiology.java:673-675
findings.recommendation: Reorder the upload guards to legacy sequence: resolve the order first (404), then count==5, then status, then size cap. If the as-built status-before-count ordering inside canAttach() is intentional, split the gate so the count check precedes the status check to match legacy verbatim selection, and correct the javadoc. Add ITs for the overlapping-error inputs (oversize+wrong-status, oversize+missing-order, 5-attachments+wrong-status) asserting the legacy message wins.

findings.title: C7 download VERIFIED-gate is a net-new behaviour (legacy download is ungated) carrying an incorrect legacy citation (line 6021/6154 is the DELETE gate, not download)
findings.detail: The as-built blocks attachment download unless the parent order is VERIFIED (LabTestService.downloadAttachment throws 422 'Could not download. Lab test is not verified'; RadiologyService 'Could not download. Radiology is not verified'), and the domain helper canDownloadAttachment() cites PatientResource.java:6021/6154. However the legacy download methods (lab PatientResource.java:5960-6007, radiology 6093-6140) perform NO status check at all — they stream the file at any order status. Lines 6021/6154 are the VERIFIED guard on the attachment-DELETE methods, not download. So this is a behaviour ADDITION presented as legacy parity with a mis-cited source. It is plausibly the safer behaviour and the increment task brief lists a download VERIFIED-gate, but it is not in the ratified-deviations list and the spec C7 text (03-INC06A-BUILD-SPEC.md:20) does not call it out. It should be ratified explicitly, not silently introduced under a wrong citation.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/LabTestService.java (downloadAttachment, ~line 616-631 in diff); RadiologyService.java (downloadAttachment, ~1115-1130 in diff); LabTest.java:674-682; Radiology.java:686-694
findings.recommendation: Either (a) record the download VERIFIED-gate as an explicit ratified deviation (owner/HDE sign-off) and fix the code/javadoc citations to stop claiming PatientResource.java:6021/6154 as the download source; or (b) if strict parity is required, remove the download status gate to match the ungated legacy download. Recommend (a) — the gate is a reasonable PHI-safety tightening — but it must be ratified, not mis-cited.

findings.title: C2 cancel-cascade javadoc mis-cites legacy: cancel_consultation does NOT cascade to child orders (cited lines 434-494 are the change_type endpoint, with no credit notes/reference)
findings.detail: ConsultationLifecycleService.cancel() now calls cancelUnsettledChildOrders(c, REF_CANCEL_CONSULTATION, ctx), with the comment 'Legacy cancel (PatientResource.java:434-494) removes PENDING child orders with UNPAID/null bills together with their bills'. But the legacy cancel_consultation (PatientResource.java:605-678) cancels ONLY the consultation's own bill (627), refunds/credit-notes only that bill (636-655), and does NO child-order iteration. Lines 434-494 are inside the change_type (OUTSIDER→OUTPATIENT) endpoint iterating NonConsultations, and they hard-delete child rows WITHOUT raising any credit note or stamping any reference. The child-order cascade that does exist (free_consultation, 701-754) flips child bills UNPAID→CANCELED with NO credit note and NO reference either. So adding a 'Canceled consultation'-referenced cascade to the cancel path is a deliberate behaviour ADDITION (the build-spec ITEM6 itself says 'cancel does NOT [cascade] — wire it'), which is fine as a spec-mandated change, but the in-code citation is wrong and implies false legacy parity. Behaviourally the addition is sound: for the normal unsettled (settled=false) child the bill has no RECEIVED payment, so cancelCharge only soft-cancels it and raises no spurious credit note, matching legacy free-path effect.
findings.recommendation: Correct the javadoc to state plainly that legacy cancel_consultation does NOT cascade and that ITEM6 adds the cascade by ratified decision (cite the spec, not PatientResource.java:434-494). No code change required — the soft-cancel/credit-note behaviour is correct.

findings.title: C7 storage filename uses System.nanoTime()+CREATE_NEW (no randomness) — theoretical collision yields 500 instead of legacy REPLACE_EXISTING overwrite
findings.detail: LocalDiskFileStorage.generateStorageName() = prefix+ownerUid+'-'+System.nanoTime()+ext, written with StandardOpenOption.CREATE_NEW. Two uploads to the same order within the same nanoTime tick (platform resolution dependent) would collide; CREATE_NEW then throws FileAlreadyExistsException → UncheckedIOException → 500. Legacy used Math.random()+timestamp with REPLACE_EXISTING (PatientServiceImpl.java:2873/2882), so it never failed on collision. Probability is negligible (ownerUid + nanoTime), and CREATE_NEW is the safer choice security-wise (no silent overwrite), so this is informational, not a defect.
findings.recommendation: Optionally append a short random/UUID suffix to the generated name to make collision impossible, or retry on FileAlreadyExistsException. Keep CREATE_NEW. Low priority.

findings.title: C7 disk write occurs inside the upload @Transactional before the row save — DB rollback after the write leaves an orphaned file
findings.detail: uploadAttachment writes bytes via fileStoragePort.store() and then persists the attachment row, all inside one @Transactional. The filesystem write is not transactional; if the subsequent DB commit fails, the file remains on disk with no row pointing at it (the best-effort unlink-on-delete only triggers on an explicit row delete). This mirrors the legacy non-transactional flow and the orphan window is small, so it is acceptable, but worth noting for the storage volume's housekeeping story.
findings.recommendation: Acceptable as-is for legacy parity. If desired later, register a TransactionSynchronization afterCompletion(STATUS_ROLLED_BACK) to unlink the just-written file, or move the disk write to after commit. Not blocking.

findings.title: addAttachment (JSON path) is a synthetic non-legacy endpoint whose error messages were aligned to the legacy MULTIPART messages
findings.detail: The pre-existing JSON addAttachment endpoint (no legacy analogue — legacy only had multipart upload) had its guard messages changed to the verbatim legacy multipart strings ('Can only attach for collected/accepted tests', 'Can not add more than 5 attachments'). This is harmless and improves consistency, but note these messages are golden-master strings that belong to the multipart path; the JSON path itself has no legacy parity contract. Confirming this is intentional and not a source of future confusion.
findings.recommendation: No action required; confirm with the team that retaining a non-legacy JSON attachment endpoint alongside the new legacy-parity multipart endpoint is intended. If the JSON path is redundant, consider deprecating it in a later increment.

review.summary:
I reviewed the full INC-06A diff against the build spec, the ADR-0008 addendum, and the legacy source of truth (PatientResource.java + PatientServiceImpl.java). The increment is largely sound and the ratified deviations are implemented correctly.

Verified GOOD: (1) Verbatim delete-guard messages match legacy exactly — 'Could not delete, only a PENDING lab test/radiology/procedure can be deleted' (PatientResource.java:2918/3424/3479). (2) Credit-note reference labels are verbatim — 'Canceled lab test'/'Canceled radiology'/'Canceled procedure'/'Canceled consultation'/'Freed consultation' (2930/3436/3503/646/free-path). (3) C1 cancelCharge runs in-tx BEFORE the order delete via Propagation.REQUIRED; CreditNoteService.doCancel soft-cancels the bill, refunds+credit-notes only on a RECEIVED payment, and applies the CR-10 fix (parent invoice deleted only when empty) — the j=j++ bug is correctly NOT reproduced. (4) C3 save_reason_for_rejection guard + verbatim 'Could not save. Only allowed for rejected tests' match legacy (2030/2046); the 422-not-409 deviation is the ratified convention. (5) C4 BillingQueries.getBillStatus is a clean read-seam: returns the already-published BillStatus named-interface enum, impl package-private in billing.application, REQUIRED read-only propagation, clinical imports only billing.api + the published BillStatus; the ADR-0008 §6 addendum is committed and correctly scoped (add_report only). No new module edge/cycle. (6) C5 bill-gate {PAID,COVERED,VERIFIED} + verbatim 'Could not add report. Payment not verified' match legacy add_report exactly (3191/3389); legacy has no order-status guard, confirming the C6 amend split is the ratified ITEM4 deviation (audited-amend vs silent overwrite). (7) The hand-written LabTestDto/RadiologyDto mappers are field-order- and type-correct: the four new amendment fields (priorReport, reportAmendedByUserUid, reportAmendedOnDayUid:String, reportAmendedAt:Instant) are inserted at matching positions in both the record and the positional constructor call (LabTestMapper:58-61, RadiologyMapper:62-65, with the radiology hasAttachment boolean correctly following). (8) V38 migration columns match the entity @Column definitions.

No BLOCKERS. The verdict is APPROVE_WITH_GAPS driven by one MAJOR functional/parity gap (F1: the 10 MiB upload cap is effectively ~1 MB because Spring's default multipart resolver limit is unconfigured, so legal 1–10 MiB uploads are rejected before the in-code verbatim gate, and the cap IT masks this). Plus three MINOR exact-process/citation issues: F2 (upload guard ORDER diverges from legacy count→status→size, producing wrong verbatim messages on overlapping-error inputs, and the 'legacy-parity' javadoc is incorrect), F3 (download VERIFIED-gate is a net-new behaviour with a mis-cited legacy line and needs explicit ratification), F4 (C2 cascade javadoc mis-cites legacy — cancel_consultation does not cascade; the addition is spec-mandated and behaviourally sound but the citation implies false parity). Two NITs (F5 nanoTime/CREATE_NEW collision→500; F6 non-transactional disk-write orphan window) and one informational NIT (F7). I recommend fixing F1 before merge (it silently breaks the legacy upload-size contract); F2–F4 are documentation/edge-case corrections that should be addressed but are not merge-blocking. No financial-total or insurance-pricing logic is altered by this increment; no PHI logging was introduced; clinical stays authentication-only as ratified (no new RBAC), which is consistent with the spec note.

verification.note:
Adversarial re-verification against the legacy source of truth, the INC06A build spec, and the as-built code confirms ALL SEVEN findings as real and in-scope. None is refuted by a ratified deviation or masked by an existing test. Verdict alignment: APPROVE_WITH_GAPS is sound; F1 is the one finding I would treat as merge-relevant.

F1 (MAJOR, CONFIRMED, merge-relevant): The 10 MiB legacy-parity cap is effectively unreachable in production. There is NO spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size / max-request-size anywhere (verified application.yml ends at hmis.attachments.max-file-size-bytes:10485760; application-test.yml has none), and NO MultipartConfigElement / MultipartResolver / MultipartProperties bean exists in backend/src/main, so Spring Boot's default 1 MB-per-file / 10 MB-per-request resolver limit binds. Files in the legal 1 MiB-10 MiB band (legacy explicitly allows up to 10485760 — verified PatientServiceImpl.java:2836-2840 lab / 2935-2939 rad) are rejected by the resolver with MaxUploadSizeExceededException BEFORE reaching the in-code gate. There is also NO @ExceptionHandler for MaxUploadSizeExceededException/MultipartException anywhere, so such uploads never return the verbatim 422 "File exceeds maximum file size allowed" and never return 201 — they fall through to the generic handler. AttachmentUploadCapIT (d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/test/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/AttachmentUploadCapIT.java:102-108) uses an 11-byte file against a 10-byte @TestPropertySource override — both far below 1 MB — so the resolver never trips and the gap is masked. The effective real-world cap is ~1 MB, a genuine behaviour/parity regression. Recommendation stands: add spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size/max-request-size >= 10 MiB so the in-code gate binds, plus an IT in the 1-10 MiB band asserting 201 and one above 10 MiB asserting the verbatim 422.

F2 (MINOR, CONFIRMED): Upload guard ORDER diverges from legacy. Legacy order is count==5 (PatientServiceImpl.java:2828 lab / 2927 rad) -> status (2832 / 2931) -> size (2838 / 2937). As-built (LabTestService.java:616-633, RadiologyService.java:576-593) is size-first, then status-before-count inside canAttach(). The spec C7 row (03-INC06A-BUILD-SPEC.md:20) explicitly requires "exact guard order", so this is a stated fidelity requirement, NOT a ratified deviation. On overlapping-error inputs (oversize+wrong-status, oversize+missing-order -> 422 instead of legacy 404, 5-attachments+wrong-status) the wrong verbatim message/status is returned. The javadoc claim "The legacy gate checks status first then count; the same order is preserved here" (LabTestService.java:602) is factually incorrect — legacy checks count at 2828 before status at 2832.

F3 (MINOR, CONFIRMED): Download VERIFIED-gate is net-new. Legacy lab download getLabTestAttachment (PatientResource.java:5960-6007) and radiology getRadiologyAttachment (6093-6140) perform NO status check — they stream at any status. Lines 6021 (lab) / 6154 (rad) are the attachment-DELETE VERIFIED guards, not download. The as-built downloadAttachment (RadiologyService.java:617-626; LabTestService download equiv.) blocks unless VERIFIED and the javadoc cites :6154/:6021 as the download source — a mis-citation. The gate is a reasonable PHI-safety tightening but is not in the ratified-deviations list and is not in the spec C7 text; it should be ratified explicitly and the citation corrected.

F4 (MINOR, CONFIRMED): C2 cascade javadoc mis-cites legacy. Legacy cancel_consultation (PatientResource.java:605-678) cancels ONLY the consultation's own bill (627), credit-notes only that bill ("Canceled consultation" ref at 646), and does NO child-order iteration (the j=j++ CR-10 bug is visible at 668). The cited lines 434-494 are inside the change_type endpoint iterating NonConsultation orders (findAllByNonConsultation at 434; message "Can not change patient type" at 494), which hard-delete child rows WITHOUT any credit note or reference. The as-built ConsultationLifecycleService.java:254-259 and 422-425 claim legacy cancel cascades "PatientResource.java:434-494" — false parity implication. The cascade itself is spec-mandated (ITEM6: "cancel does NOT [cascade] — wire it") and behaviourally sound (unsettled child bills have no RECEIVED payment, so cancelCharge only soft-cancels with no spurious credit note); only the citation needs correcting. No code change required.

F5 (NIT, CONFIRMED): generateStorageName uses prefix+ownerUid+"-"+System.nanoTime()+ext with StandardOpenOption.CREATE_NEW (LocalDiskFileStorage.java:167-172, 76). A same-tick collision -> FileAlreadyExistsException -> UncheckedIOException -> 500, where legacy used Math.random()+timestamp + REPLACE_EXISTING and never failed. Probability negligible; CREATE_NEW is the safer no-silent-overwrite choice. Informational only; reviewer's own classification (low priority) is correct. (Note: the finding's line citations 1995-2000/1904 are diff-relative; the actual file is 246 lines — substance is accurate.)

F6 (NIT, CONFIRMED): uploadAttachment writes bytes via fileStoragePort.store() before the row save, all inside one @Transactional (LabTestService.java:611-645, RadiologyService.java:571-604). A DB rollback after the non-transactional disk write leaves an orphaned file (unlink-on-delete only fires on explicit row delete). Mirrors legacy non-transactional flow; small orphan window. Acceptable as-is per the reviewer; informational.

F7 (NIT/informational, CONFIRMED): The pre-existing JSON addAttachment endpoint (no legacy multipart analogue) reuses the verbatim legacy multipart guard strings (LabTestService.java:576-581, RadiologyService.java:537-542). Harmless and consistent; the reviewer asks only to confirm intent. Accurate observation, no action required.

Cross-cutting confirmations of the reviewer's GOOD list spot-checked and sound: verbatim delete-guard/credit-note reference labels match legacy; C4 BillingQueries.getBillStatus read-seam is clean (no new module edge/cycle); C5 bill-gate {PAID,COVERED,VERIFIED} + verbatim "Could not add report. Payment not verified" match legacy add_report (PatientResource.java:3195/3393); ratified deviations (soft-flag cancel, CR-10 fix, 422-not-409, opaque filename, path-traversal hardening, unlink-on-delete, ADR-0008 §6 relaxation, audited-amend, authentication-only) are correctly implemented and not re-flagged. No financial-total or insurance-pricing logic is altered; no PHI logging introduced; clinical stays authentication-only as ratified.

Net: 7 confirmed, 0 rejected. F1 should be addressed before merge (silent break of the legacy upload-size contract); F2-F4 are exact-process/citation corrections to address (F4 doc-only); F5-F7 are informational.

Relevant files (absolute): d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/main/resources/application.yml; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/test/resources/application-test.yml; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/test/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/AttachmentUploadCapIT.java; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/LabTestService.java; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/RadiologyService.java; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/ConsultationLifecycleService.java; d:/My_Works/HMS/HMSCLEAN2/backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/shared/storage/LocalDiskFileStorage.java; D:/My_Works/HMS/ZANAHMIS-2-feature/Zana-HMIS-API/api/api/src/main/java/com/orbix/api/service/PatientServiceImpl.java; D:/My_Works/HMS/ZANAHMIS-2-feature/Zana-HMIS-API/api/api/src/main/java/com/orbix/api/api/PatientResource.java.

review.lens: QA / Test Engineer — behaviour-parity, guard-message verbatim, credit-note assertion completeness, test isolation
review.summary: inc-06A is substantively well-tested. The critical cancel-gate messages, bill-gate addReport flow, amend-path happy path, download VERIFIED-gate, and upload cap are all present with verbatim detail assertions. Seven specific gaps require changes before sign-off: (1) C1 credit-note assertions are complete for lab but absent for radiology and procedure deletes. (2) C2 refactored cancelUnsettledChildOrders now takes a reference parameter; neither the cancel path ("Canceled consultation") nor the free path ("Freed consultation") has a test asserting the credit-note reference label stamped on cascade-cancelled child bills. (3) C7 guard order is inverted from legacy: legacy checks count/status before size cap; as-built checks size cap first — this is a parity deviation that is misattributed as "legacy-parity" in the spec comments and is untested (no test for the count-before-cap or status-before-cap scenario). (4) The amend-triplet assertion in both LabTestIT and RadiologyIT omits reportAmendedOnDayUid, leaving one of three amend-triplet columns unverified. (5) AttachmentUploadCapIT covers only the lab path; no cap test for radiology. (6) No unit tests for LocalDiskFileStorage path-traversal hardening (the only security-hardening code added in the increment). (7) BillingQueriesIT only tests UNPAID status; COVERED and VERIFIED pass-cases for the live seam are not directly asserted (though covered indirectly by add_report IT flows).

findings.title: C1: No credit-note reference-label or bill-cancellation assertions for radiology and procedure delete
findings.detail: LabTestIT.java (around diff line 2598) asserts bill→CANCELED, payment→REFUNDED, and credit-note reference='Canceled lab test' for the paid-lab-delete path (C1 ITEM1). RadiologyIT and ProcedureIT do NOT have equivalent assertions. The delete-happy-path tests (RadiologyIT.delete_pending_204, ProcedureIT.delete_pending_204) only assert HTTP 204 and repository empty. The bill soft-cancel and credit-note seam wired for radiology (REF_CANCEL_RADIOLOGY='Canceled radiology', RadiologyService.java in diff at line 1013) and procedure (REF_CANCEL_PROCEDURE='Canceled procedure', ProcedureService.java in diff at line 714) are exercised in CI only by the billing unit tests in CreditNoteIT, not by the clinical delete path end-to-end. Legacy reference labels at PatientResource.java:3436 and :3503 are confirmed correct, but the radiology and procedure delete integration paths have no assertion that cancelCharge is actually called with the right reference.
findings.recommendation: Add a test in RadiologyIT and ProcedureIT that (a) seeds a PENDING order with a PAID bill (pay at cashier), (b) deletes the order, (c) asserts bill→CANCELED, payment→REFUNDED, and a PENDING credit-note with reference 'Canceled radiology'/'Canceled procedure' exactly matching the legacy strings. Mirror the pattern in LabTestIT.delete_paidPendingLabTest_reversesBill_refunds_raisesCreditNote.

findings.title: C2: No test asserts the credit-note reference label on the cancel-cascade and free-cascade child bills
findings.detail: The refactored cancelUnsettledChildOrders(consultation, reference, ctx) now takes a reference label parameter — 'Canceled consultation' for the cancel path and 'Freed consultation' for the free path (ConsultationLifecycleService.java, diff lines 130 and 140). The new C2 test (ConsultationLifecycleIT.cancel_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills) asserts only BillStatus.CANCELED for unsettled child bills; it does not assert that the credit-note reference label is 'Canceled consultation'. The free-path cascade (REF_FREED_CONSULTATION) has no integration test at all that seeds a child order and verifies the cascade fires. If the parameter were swapped, the wrong reference would silently reach the credit note with no test catching it.
findings.recommendation: Extend cancel_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills to also assert a PatientCreditNote exists with reference='Canceled consultation' for the unsettled child bill that had a RECEIVED payment. Add a symmetric free_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills test (seed a PAID child lab bill on an IN_PROCESS consultation, call free, assert credit-note reference='Freed consultation').

findings.title: C7: Guard order inverted from legacy — size cap checked before count/status, but tests only assert cap, not the legacy ordering
findings.detail: Legacy PatientServiceImpl.java:2827-2839 (lab) and :2926-2938 (radiology) check (1) count==5, (2) status wrong, (3) file size over cap, in that order. The as-built uploadAttachment in LabTestService.java (diff line 611-632) and RadiologyService.java (diff line 1076-1092) check (1) size cap first, then (2) count/status. The build spec's own docstring says 'Guard order (legacy-parity): (1) size cap' which misattributes the inversion as parity. AttachmentUploadCapIT only tests that the cap rejects a file — it does not test that a status-wrong request also fails with the right message, and does not test the count=5 limit. The inversion is undetected because no test calls uploadAttachment on a wrong-status order with a file that simultaneously exceeds the cap.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/application/LabTestService.java:611 (uploadAttachment, size cap check before requireLabTest) and RadiologyService.java:1076; backend/src/test/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/AttachmentUploadCapIT.java:77
findings.recommendation: Decide: either accept the guard-order inversion as a ratified deviation (document it explicitly alongside the other approved deviations and add a comment in the code citing the legacy order and why it was inverted) OR restore the legacy order (count first, status second, cap third). Either way, add a test that calls uploadAttachment on a not-COLLECTED lab test with a small file (below cap) and asserts 422 with 'Can only attach for collected tests', and a separate test with a 5-attachment lab test and a small file asserting 422 with 'Can not add more than 5 attachments'. These guard paths are currently exercised only via the JSON addAttachment endpoint, not the multipart uploadAttachment endpoint.

findings.title: C6: reportAmendedOnDayUid not asserted in either lab or radiology amend tests
findings.detail: LabTest.amendReport and Radiology.amendReport both stamp three fields: reportAmendedByUserUid, reportAmendedOnDayUid, and reportAmendedAt (diff LabTest.java:1298-1300, Radiology.java:1386-1389). The amend-path tests in LabTestIT.verifiedReport_blockedViaAddReport_amendableViaAmendPath_retainsPrior (diff line 2847) and RadiologyIT.verifiedReport_blockedViaAddReport_amendableViaAmendPath_retainsPrior (diff line 3043) assert reportAmendedByUserUid and reportAmendedAt but omit reportAmendedOnDayUid. The dayUid is always the current business day; if the dayUid were never set (null) the test would still pass.
findings.recommendation: Add .andExpect(jsonPath('$.reportAmendedOnDayUid').isNotEmpty()) to both amend happy-path tests to complete the amend-triplet assertion.

findings.title: C7: AttachmentUploadCapIT only covers the lab upload cap; radiology upload cap has no test
findings.detail: AttachmentUploadCapIT.uploadAttachment_overCap_422 (diff line 2308) drives a lab test to COLLECTED and then uploads an over-cap file. The radiology uploadAttachment endpoint (RadiologyController, diff line 1659) has the same cap guard (diff RadiologyService.java:1077) but no equivalent test covers the 422 path for radiology. Existing RadiologyIT tests do not use @TestPropertySource to set a tiny cap.
findings.recommendation: Add a second @Test to AttachmentUploadCapIT for the radiology upload cap: drive a radiology order to ACCEPTED, then upload an 11-byte file with the 10-byte cap config active, and assert 422 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed'.

findings.title: C7: No unit tests for LocalDiskFileStorage path-traversal hardening
findings.detail: LocalDiskFileStorage.guardNoTraversal and guardInsideBase (diff lines 2051-2072) are the only security-hardening code added in this increment, approved by the security architect. There are no unit or integration tests that call store/read/delete with traversal inputs (e.g., '../etc/passwd', filenames with '/' or backslash) and assert 422 InvalidPatientOperationException. The IT tests that exercise upload and download use only well-formed filenames. If the traversal guard were accidentally removed, no test would catch it.
findings.recommendation: Add a LocalDiskFileStorageTest unit test (plain JUnit 5, no Spring context) with at least three cases: (a) store with '../malicious' as prefix or ownerUid results in InvalidPatientOperationException; (b) read with '../../etc/passwd' as storageFilename results in InvalidPatientOperationException; (c) delete with a traversal filename results in InvalidPatientOperationException. Use a temp directory for the base path.

findings.title: C4: BillingQueriesIT only tests UNPAID status; no direct assertion that seam returns COVERED or VERIFIED
findings.detail: BillingQueriesIT.getBillStatus_returnsLiveStatus (diff line 2203) creates a CASH bill which defaults to UNPAID and asserts UNPAID. The three statuses that the add_report gate accepts are PAID, COVERED, VERIFIED. None of these is directly tested in BillingQueriesIT. The PAID path is exercised indirectly (LabTestIT.saveResult_andAddReport_billGated pays at cashier then succeeds), and COVERED is exercised by INSURANCE add_report tests. VERIFIED as a bill status (insurance claims workflow) has no add_report test at all. If getBillStatus returned a stale/wrong enum for COVERED or VERIFIED, the add_report gate IT would still fail-safe (it would block), so the gap is mostly in asserting the seam's correctness for all three admitted statuses.
findings.recommendation: Out of scope for inc-06A (VERIFIED bill-status originates from the insurance-claims increment, not yet built). Document this as a known gap to be closed when the claims increment delivers VERIFIED bill transitions. The PAID and COVERED paths are adequately covered by end-to-end IT flows.

findings.title: C2: cancel cascade test seeds only lab-test child orders; radiology/procedure/prescription cascade is untested
findings.detail: ConsultationLifecycleIT.cancel_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills seeds two lab tests (one unsettled, one settled) and asserts the cascade. The cancelUnsettledChildOrders method iterates lab → radiology → procedure → prescription (ConsultationLifecycleService.java, diff lines 171-198). The radiology, procedure, and prescription legs of the cascade have no integration test asserting they are skipped for settled orders or cancelled for unsettled ones. Probability of divergence is low (the code is a loop over four repositories with the same pattern), but one broken canAttach call on a different entity type would not be caught.
findings.recommendation: Either extend cancel_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills to also seed one unsettled radiology and one unsettled procedure child order (using the same repository-direct seeding pattern as the lab tests) and assert both are CANCELED, or add a comment acknowledging the loop-coverage gap as accepted.

rejectedFindings: F-07: Correctly self-identified by the original reviewer as out of scope. COVERED and VERIFIED bill statuses cannot be directly exercised by BillingQueriesIT because the insurance-claims increment that transitions bills to VERIFIED has not yet been built. The PAID path is indirectly asserted by LabTestIT.saveResult_andAddReport_billGated (pays at cashier then calls add_report); the COVERED path is exercised by the INSURANCE add_report integration test. No new test infrastructure exists in this increment to drive a bill to VERIFIED or COVERED from within the billing module in isolation, so the gap is legitimately deferred.
verification.note:
Seven of eight findings survive adversarial re-check. Evidence for each confirmed finding follows.

F-01 (MAJOR — confirmed). Production code in RadiologyService.java and ProcedureService.java correctly calls billingCommands.cancelCharge(..., REF_CANCEL_RADIOLOGY, ctx) and billingCommands.cancelCharge(..., REF_CANCEL_PROCEDURE, ctx) before hard-deleting the order row. However, RadiologyIT.delete_pending_204_deleteNonPending_422 and ProcedureIT.delete_pending_204 only verify HTTP 204 and repository empty for a CASH order that never had a RECEIVED payment. No test seeds a PAID bill (RECEIVED payment status) then deletes the PENDING order and asserts (a) bill→CANCELED, (b) payment→REFUNDED, (c) a PENDING PatientCreditNote with reference exactly 'Canceled radiology' / 'Canceled procedure'. The LabTestIT.delete_paidPendingLabTest_reversesBill_refunds_raisesCreditNote test at line 453 does all three assertions for lab, so there is a clear pattern to follow, but radiology and procedure have no equivalent. CreditNoteIT at line 263 calls cancelCharge directly with 'Canceled procedure' as a billing-unit test — it does not exercise the clinical delete HTTP path end-to-end.

F-02 (MAJOR — confirmed). ConsultationLifecycleIT.cancel_cascadesToUnsettledChildOrderBills at line 279-285 asserts patientBillRepository.findByUid(unsettledLabBillUid).getStatus() == BillStatus.CANCELED. It does not assert that a PatientCreditNote exists with reference='Canceled consultation' for the bill that had a RECEIVED payment. The reference parameter was the central point of the C2 refactoring: the old single-path method took no parameter; the refactored version takes 'Canceled consultation' for cancel and 'Freed consultation' for free. Swapping those strings would produce a credit-note with the wrong label and no test would catch it. Additionally, the free path has no child-order cascade test: free_inProcess_transitionsToSignedOut (line 311) and free_transfered_transitionsToSignedOut (line 329) seed no child orders and make no assertions about cascade or credit-note reference. Legacy PatientResource.java:701-754 (free_consultation) only sets child-order bills to CANCELED without creating credit notes; the as-built uses cancelCharge which creates credit notes — this extension is within the ratified soft-flag standard but makes the reference label correctness more important to test, not less.

F-03 (MAJOR — confirmed). Legacy PatientServiceImpl.java:2827-2839 (lab) and :2926-2938 (radiology) check guards in this order: (1) count==5 → 'Can not add more than 5 attachments', (2) status wrong → 'Can only attach for collected/accepted tests', (3) file.getSize() > maxFileSize → 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed'. The as-built LabTestService.uploadAttachment and RadiologyService.uploadAttachment check: (1) bytes.length > maxFileSizeBytes, (2) canAttach(count). The docstring in both services labels this inverted order as 'Guard order (legacy-parity)' — this is a factual error; the order is the opposite of legacy. The build spec for ITEM5 explicitly requires 'exact guard order'. The practical consequence: a user who uploads a file that exceeds the cap to a wrong-status order will receive 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed' from the modern system but 'Can only attach for collected tests' (or the count message) from the legacy system. AttachmentUploadCapIT only asserts that a correctly-statused (COLLECTED) order returns the cap error — it does not probe the inversion scenario. No test calls uploadAttachment on a wrong-status order with a small file (which would expose the missing 422 for the status guard), nor on a wrong-status order with an over-cap file (which would reveal which error fires first). This is an unratified parity deviation; it must be either explicitly ratified with a deviation record or the guard order must be corrected to match legacy.

F-04 (MINOR — confirmed). LabTest.amendReport stamps three fields: reportAmendedByUserUid, reportAmendedOnDayUid, reportAmendedAt (LabTest.java domain entity, lines cited in the diff). LabTestIT at line 975-976 asserts .andExpect(jsonPath('$.reportAmendedByUserUid').value('admin')) and .andExpect(jsonPath('$.reportAmendedAt').isNotEmpty()) but has no assertion for reportAmendedOnDayUid. RadiologyIT at line 458-459 has the same omission. The dayUid is always the current business day opened in setUp(); if the field were never persisted (null), the test would still pass because no assertion covers it. This leaves one of the three amend-triplet columns unverified.

F-05 (MINOR — confirmed). AttachmentUploadCapIT contains exactly one @Test method (uploadAttachment_overCap_422) which drives a lab test to COLLECTED status and uploads an 11-byte file against a 10-byte cap. The radiology upload endpoint in RadiologyController is wired to the same cap check (RadiologyService.uploadAttachment line 577: if (bytes.length > storageProperties.maxFileSizeBytes())), but no test in AttachmentUploadCapIT or RadiologyIT targets that path with an over-cap file. The IT class comment explicitly mentions lab only.

F-06 (MINOR — confirmed). Glob searches for LocalDiskFileStorageTest.java, FileStorageTest.java, and all test files in the shared.storage package return no results. The guardNoTraversal method (lines 223-231 in LocalDiskFileStorage.java) and guardInsideBase method (lines 239-244) are the only security-hardening code added in inc-06A, explicitly noted as security-architect-approved. There is no unit or integration test that calls store/read/delete with path-traversal inputs (e.g., '../etc/passwd', 'foo/bar', 'foo\\bar') and asserts InvalidPatientOperationException. If either guard were accidentally removed or weakened (e.g., during a refactor), no test would detect it. A plain JUnit 5 unit test using a temp directory requires no Spring context and is straightforward to write.

F-07 (MINOR — REJECTED as out of scope). The reviewer's own inScope=false designation is correct. VERIFIED bill-status is produced by the insurance-claims workflow, which is a future increment. No mechanism exists within inc-06A or the current billing module to transition a bill to VERIFIED in a test context. The PAID path is indirectly tested by LabTestIT end-to-end flows that pay at the cashier then call add_report. This is a documented gap to close in the claims increment.

F-08 (NIT — confirmed). cancelUnsettledChildOrders at ConsultationLifecycleService lines 436-459 iterates four collections: lab, radiology, procedure, prescription. The cancel cascade test (line 260-286) seeds only lab children (two LabTest rows). The radiology, procedure, and prescription legs of the loop have no seeded data in any test — meaning a bug specific to how any of those entity types expose their patientBillUid or isSettled flag would not be caught. The code is structurally uniform (the same pattern for each type), so the risk of divergence is low, but the gap is real. A comment in the test acknowledging the accepted loop-coverage gap, or extension of the test with one seeded radiology and procedure child, would close it.

Release sign-off is BLOCKED on F-01, F-02, and F-03 (MAJORs). F-04, F-05, F-06, and F-08 are minors/nits that must also be addressed before sign-off under standard quality gates, but the three MAJORs are the primary blockers. The F-03 guard-order inversion requires an explicit ownership decision: either correct the guard order to match legacy (count→status→cap), or raise a formal ratified-deviation record with the legacy-analyst and business-analyst before this can be signed off as 'modern design, exact process'.

review.lens: PHI/security review of INC-06A clinical-order top-up: clinical→billing read seam scope, attachment path-traversal hardening, download VERIFIED-gate (PHI leak prevention), multipart size-cap as DoS control, filename/Content-Disposition injection, audit coverage of new mutations, and the authentication-only authz model.
review.summary: The security-relevant core of INC-06A is sound. The C4 ADR-0008 §6 relaxation is genuinely narrow: BillingQueries.getBillStatus is read-only, only the already-published BillStatus enum crosses the boundary, it runs in the caller's tx, and it adds no new module edge or cycle. The path-traversal hardening in LocalDiskFileStorage is defense-in-depth (string reject of .. / \ + normalize + startsWith(basePath) + CREATE_NEW) and I could not find a bypass on the read/delete paths (where attacker-influenced names could reach). The download VERIFIED-gate (SEC-04) does prevent unverified PHI image leak and is in fact STRONGER than legacy (legacy had NO download gate at all). The audited-amend path (C6) with prior_report retention is a clear patient-safety improvement over the legacy silent overwrite, and the ratified deviations (soft-cancel, CR-10, 422-not-409, opaque filename, unlink-on-delete) are all sound. However, C7's new multipart upload + INLINE download introduces two genuine NEW risks not present (or not exploitable) in legacy: (1) no MIME allow-list on upload combined with Content-Disposition: inline and MediaTypeFactory content-typing means an uploaded .svg/.html attachment is served as active content from the app origin to any authenticated viewer = stored XSS (PHI/session theft); legacy served everything as the bogus application/* which browsers download rather than render, so this is a regression. (2) The size cap is enforced AFTER the controller calls file.getBytes(), fully materializing the upload into heap, and there is no spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size / max-request-size configured and no MultipartException/MaxUploadSizeExceededException handler, so the 10 MiB cap is not an effective DoS control and an oversize upload yields a 500 (and a non-verbatim message) instead of the verbatim 422. Also minor: the path-traversal guard reflects the raw filename back in the 422 detail, and the C7 upload guard ORDER diverges from legacy (legacy is count→status→size; as-built is size→status/count) despite the javadoc claiming legacy-parity. The authentication-only model is acceptable for this increment (consistent with the existing inc-05 clinical baseline and the ratified no-new-RBAC decision), but I am flagging the absence of any patient-scope/BOLA check on download as a known, pre-existing gap to track, not an inc-06A regression. Net: approve, but SEC-INLINE-XSS and SEC-DOS-CAP should be fixed before these endpoints are exposed to real PHI.

findings.title: Inline download of uploaded attachments with no MIME allow-list = stored XSS on the app origin
findings.detail: The new download endpoints set Content-Disposition: inline and derive the Content-Type from the stored filename via MediaTypeFactory.getMediaType(dl.fileName()) (LabTestController.java downloadAttachment ~line 1521-1534 in diff; RadiologyController.java ~1675-1688). The stored filename's extension is derived from the attacker-controlled originalFilename in LocalDiskFileStorage.deriveExtension (LocalDiskFileStorage.java:178-193) and there is NO content-type / extension allow-list on upload (LabTestService.uploadAttachment / RadiologyService.uploadAttachment only check size + status + count). An authenticated user (any role — authz is authentication-only) can upload evil.svg or evil.html to a lab/radiology order; once the order is VERIFIED, any clinician opening that attachment receives it inline with content-type image/svg+xml or text/html, executing attacker script in the HMIS origin (PHI exfiltration / token theft). Spring Security's default X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff does NOT help here because the declared content-type IS html/svg. Legacy is NOT actually vulnerable the same way: it serves response.setContentType("application/*") (PatientResource.java:5993, 6126), a bogus type browsers treat as a download, so the as-built 'corrected' content-typing is a regression in this respect.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/web/LabTestController.java (downloadAttachment) and RadiologyController.java (downloadAttachment); upload path LocalDiskFileStorage.java:178-193 + LabTestService.java uploadAttachment ~590, RadiologyService.java uploadAttachment ~554
findings.recommendation: For the inline download response, force a safe content type for non-image clinical attachments (serve application/octet-stream, or restrict inline rendering to a fixed image allow-list e.g. png/jpeg/pdf and use Content-Disposition: attachment for everything else), and/or enforce an upload MIME/extension allow-list. At minimum add Content-Disposition: attachment (not inline) for any type outside {image/png,image/jpeg,application/pdf}. Confirm Spring Security default headers (nosniff) are retained. This is a clinical-image PHI surface so it should be closed before real-data exposure.

findings.title: Upload size cap is not an effective DoS control: full byte[] materialized before the check, no multipart limits, wrong status on overflow
findings.detail: The controller calls file.getBytes() to extract the whole upload into a heap byte[] BEFORE the service checks bytes.length > maxFileSizeBytes (LabTestController.uploadAttachment passes file.getBytes(); LabTestService.uploadAttachment:576-580 checks the cap only after the bytes already exist; same for radiology). The cap therefore cannot prevent the memory/allocation cost it is meant to bound. There is no spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size or max-request-size configured (application.yml has only hmis.attachments.max-file-size-bytes), so Spring's defaults (1MB file / 10MB request) govern the wire — meaning a >1MB legitimate upload may fail at the framework BEFORE reaching the verbatim-422 logic, and a large multipart still buffers to disk/temp. Critically there is NO @ExceptionHandler for MultipartException / MaxUploadSizeExceededException (grep: none in the codebase), so an oversize/ malformed multipart hits the catch-all 500 (GlobalExceptionHandler.handleUnexpected) instead of the legacy-verbatim 422 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed'. Legacy checks file.getSize() (PatientServiceImpl.java:2838,2937) — stream metadata, no full read — so the as-built is a DoS regression and a parity break on the error path.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/clinical/web/LabTestController.java uploadAttachment (file.getBytes()); LabTestService.java:576-580 / RadiologyService.java:~1077 cap check; application.yml:44-50 (no spring.servlet.multipart limits); no MultipartException handler in GlobalExceptionHandler.java
findings.recommendation: Check size from MultipartFile.getSize() (metadata) at the controller/service boundary BEFORE getBytes(), mirroring legacy. Set spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size to align with the 10 MiB cap (slightly above, to let the app emit the verbatim 422 rather than a framework error). Add an @ExceptionHandler(MaxUploadSizeExceededException/MultipartException) that returns 422 with the verbatim 'File exceeds maximum file size allowed' so the cap is both a real DoS guard and parity-correct.

findings.title: Path-traversal guard reflects raw attacker-controlled filename in the 422 problem detail
findings.detail: guardNoTraversal throws InvalidPatientOperationException("Invalid attachment filename (path traversal rejected): " + filename) (LocalDiskFileStorage.java:227-230). InvalidPatientOperationException's detail is echoed to the client by GlobalExceptionHandler.handleHmis -> toResponse (GlobalExceptionHandler.java:35-38,114-124). The reflected value is attacker-controlled. Because the response is application/problem+json and JSON-encoded, reflected-XSS is low risk, but echoing raw payloads is unnecessary and contravenes the PHI-minimisation principle (don't echo untrusted input). Note: this string path is only reachable for read/delete (where storageFilename comes from the persisted row) — store() does not call guardNoTraversal on the original filename and only derives a sanitized extension, so the message is unlikely to carry truly hostile content in practice, but the pattern should still not reflect input.
findings.recommendation: Return a static message ('Invalid attachment filename') and log the offending value server-side only. Trivial change, removes the input-reflection smell.

findings.title: C7 upload guard order diverges from legacy despite javadoc claiming legacy-parity
findings.detail: The diff and javadoc state the size cap runs 'first (legacy-parity)' (LabTestService.uploadAttachment:576, RadiologyService.uploadAttachment:1077). Legacy actually checks attachment count==5 FIRST (PatientServiceImpl.java:2828 lab, 2927 rad), THEN status (2832/2931), THEN size via file.getSize() (2838/2937). The as-built order is size -> status/count. This is an exact-process divergence on the guard ordering (observable: which 422 message wins when multiple conditions are violated). Not a security defect on its own, but it is a parity claim that is factually wrong and should either be corrected or explicitly ratified as a deviation.
findings.recommendation: Either reorder to count -> status -> size to match legacy exactly, or record the size-first order as a ratified deviation with rationale (note SEC-06A-02 already requires the size check to use getSize() up front, which makes size-first defensible — but then document it, do not call it 'legacy-parity').

findings.title: VERIFIED download-gate is a net-new control, mislabeled as legacy parity with a wrong citation
findings.detail: The download VERIFIED-gate javadoc cites PatientResource.java:6021 (lab) / 6154 (rad) as the source of the gate (LabTestPort.downloadAttachment javadoc; LabTest.canDownloadAttachment LabTest.java:676 'PatientResource.java:6021'). But 6021/6154 are the attachment-DELETE guards; the legacy DOWNLOAD endpoints (getLabTestAttachment 5960-6007, getRadiologyAttachment 6093-6140) have NO status gate and stream the file unconditionally once authenticated. So the VERIFIED download-gate is a genuine PHI-hardening improvement (good — keep it), but it is net-new, not parity, and the citation is incorrect. Per exact-process discipline, net-new controls should be flagged/ratified rather than presented as parity.
findings.recommendation: Keep the gate (it prevents unverified-image PHI leak), but correct the legacy citation and record it as a ratified net-new security control rather than parity, so the audit trail of deviations is accurate.

findings.title: Audit records capture who/what/when but no before/after for the new mutations (amend, addReport, cancelCharge, upload, delete)
findings.detail: AuditRecorder.record persists only (entityType, entityUid, action, actor, occurredAt) + a checksum (AuditRecorder.java:33-44); it does not store before/after values. All new inc-06A mutations call this single-line recorder: amendReport/addReport/saveRejectComment/delete (LabTestService, RadiologyService), upload/deleteAttachment, and the cancelCharge cascade audits the consultation UPDATE only. For the report-amend case this is partially compensated because the row itself retains prior_report + the amend triplet (an in-row append-only trail), which is good. But for cancelCharge, attachment upload/delete, and reject-comment edits there is no field-level before/after in the audit log. This is the inc-05/ADR-0007 audit design baseline, NOT an inc-06A regression, so it is out of strict inc-06A scope to change — but per the security-architect audit-completeness mandate it should be tracked as a known limitation, especially for financial (cancelCharge/credit-note) and clinical (report) mutations.
findings.location: backend/src/main/java/com/otapp/hmis/shared/audit/AuditRecorder.java:33-44; callers across LabTestService.java / RadiologyService.java / ConsultationLifecycleService.java (inc-06A diff)
findings.recommendation: Track as an audit-architecture item: decide whether the compliance-driven audit scope requires before/after snapshots for clinical/financial mutations and, if so, extend the audit schema (data-architect owns physical schema). No inc-06A code change required now; the report-amend in-row trail is adequate for this increment.

findings.title: No patient-scope / object-level authorization on attachment download (BOLA/IDOR) — pre-existing, not introduced by C7
findings.detail: downloadAttachment resolves the attachment by uid and only checks parent-order VERIFIED status; there is no check that the requesting user is entitled to that patient's records (LabTestService.downloadAttachment:616-631, RadiologyService.downloadAttachment:1115-1130). Any authenticated user can enumerate attachment uids and pull any patient's VERIFIED lab/radiology image (PHI). This is consistent with the ratified authentication-only model and the legacy (which also had no object-level scoping and no download gate at all), so it is NOT an inc-06A regression and is out of scope to fix here. It is, however, a real PHI exposure surface for the modular monolith and belongs on the security backlog. ULIDs are unguessable, which mitigates blind enumeration somewhat.
findings.recommendation: Acceptable for this increment under the ratified authentication-only decision. Track as a future control: object-level authorization (patient-scope or department-scope) on PHI download once the RBAC model extends beyond authentication-only. Do not add net-new RBAC in inc-06A (would violate the no-new-RBAC ratification).

findings.title: BillingQueries seam is read-only and well-scoped, but the type-level surface is generic getBillStatus — confirm it stays add_report-only
findings.detail: The C4 relaxation is correctly narrow at the architecture level: BillingQueries.getBillStatus is read-only, returns only the already-published BillStatus enum, runs Propagation.REQUIRED in the caller's tx, is package-private impl, and adds no module edge/cycle (ModularityTest GREEN) — this is a sound, minimal §6 relaxation and I approve it. The only residual: the method name/signature is generic ('get any bill's status by uid'), so nothing structurally prevents a future caller from using it for non-add_report gates that ADR-0008 §6 still forbids (the addendum relaxes §6 ONLY for add_report). The two current callers (requireBillPaidCoveredOrVerified in LabTestService/RadiologyService) are correctly the add_report+amend bill-gate only; settlement gates still use the local settled flag, as required.
findings.recommendation: No code change required. Keep an ADR/review note that BillingQueries.getBillStatus is sanctioned for the add_report/amend bill-gate ONLY; any future caller must be reviewed against the §6 addendum scope. Optionally add an Architecture/ArchUnit test asserting only the clinical report-gate path references BillingQueries to lock the scope.

rejectedFindings: SEC-06A-06 — REJECTED as an inc-06A finding: out of scope and correctly self-marked inScope:false. Verified AuditRecorder.record (AuditRecorder.java:33-44) persists only (entityType, entityUid, action, actor, occurredAt, checksum) with no before/after, but this is the pre-existing inc-05/ADR-0007 audit baseline applied uniformly, NOT introduced by inc-06A. The report-amend path additionally retains prior_report + amend triplet in-row (an append-only trail), which is adequate for this increment. Track as an audit-architecture backlog item for data-architect; no inc-06A code change.

SEC-06A-07 — REJECTED as an inc-06A finding: out of scope and correctly self-marked inScope:false. Verified downloadAttachment (LabTestService.java:656-671, RadiologyService.java:615-630) only checks parent-order VERIFIED, no patient/object-level scope. But legacy had NO object scoping AND no download gate at all (PatientResource.java:5960-6007 / 6093-6140 confirmed), and this is consistent with the ratified authentication-only / no-new-RBAC decision. Not an inc-06A regression; ULIDs mitigate blind enumeration. Belongs on the security backlog, not an inc-06A defect. Adding RBAC now would itself violate the no-new-RBAC ratification.

SEC-06A-08 — REJECTED as a finding (no actionable defect): this is an architectural approval, not a defect. Verified BillingQueries.getBillStatus (BillingQueries.java:27-37) is read-only, returns only the already-published BillStatus enum, runs in the caller's tx, has exactly two callers both being the add_report/amend bill-gate (requireBillPaidCoveredOrVerified in LabTestService.java:473-480 / RadiologyService.java:446), and adds no module edge/cycle. The §6 relaxation is genuinely narrow and sound. The finding's only ask (an ADR/review note + optional ArchUnit lock) is a hardening suggestion, not a bug — recorded as advisory, not a confirmed finding.
verification.note:
Verified against as-built code, the inc-06A diff, and the legacy source. CONFIRMED (5): SEC-06A-01, -02, -03, -04, -05. REJECTED (3): SEC-06A-06, -07 (out-of-scope pre-existing baselines, correctly self-marked inScope:false), -08 (an approval/advisory note, not a defect).

KEY EVIDENCE:
- SEC-06A-01 (MAJOR, real): LabTestController.downloadAttachment lines 403-410 (and RadiologyController 405-412) set Content-Disposition: inline + MediaTypeFactory.getMediaType(dl.fileName()). The stored filename's extension is derived from attacker-controlled originalFilename via LocalDiskFileStorage.deriveExtension (178-193); upload has size+status+count gates only — NO MIME/extension allow-list (LabTestService.uploadAttachment 611-645, RadiologyService 571-604). Legacy served response.setContentType(\"application/*\") (PatientResource.java:5993, 6126) which browsers download not render, so as-built inline+real-content-type is a NEW stored-XSS surface on clinical/PHI images. (Nuance: the Content-Disposition filename is the opaque storage name, but the content-type is still derived from the real extension, so the vector stands.) Not covered by any test (ITs only upload .pdf).
- SEC-06A-02 (MAJOR, real): controller does file.getBytes() (full heap materialization) BEFORE the bytes.length cap check (LabTestService 617, RadiologyService 577); legacy used file.getSize() metadata (PatientServiceImpl 2838/2937). application.yml has hmis.attachments.max-file-size-bytes only — NO spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size/max-request-size, so Spring defaults (1MB file/10MB req) govern the wire; a 1-10MB upload trips the framework first. NO MaxUploadSizeExceededException/MultipartException handler in GlobalExceptionHandler → catch-all 500 (line 107), not the verbatim 422. AttachmentUploadCapIT masks this (10-byte configured cap, 11-byte file never reaches Spring's 1MB default).
- SEC-06A-03 (MINOR, real): LocalDiskFileStorage.guardNoTraversal:227-230 reflects raw '+ filename' into the problem+json detail. Low practical exploitability (store() doesn't guard the original name; read/delete use the persisted opaque storage name) but the input-reflection pattern is real — return a static message, log server-side.
- SEC-06A-04 (MINOR, real): legacy guard order is count→status→size using file.getSize() (PatientServiceImpl 2828/2832/2838 lab; 2927/2931/2937 rad); as-built is size→status/count with the javadoc explicitly claiming 'Guard order (legacy-parity)' / 'Size cap first'. The parity claim is factually wrong and observable (which 422 wins when multiple conditions violated). Either reorder to count→status→size, or — given SEC-06A-02 wants getSize() up front — document size-first as a ratified deviation rather than calling it 'legacy-parity'.
- SEC-06A-05 (NIT, real): legacy download (PatientResource.java:5960-6007 lab, 6093-6140 rad) has NO status gate; lines 6021/6154 are the attachment-DELETE guards. The as-built VERIFIED download-gate javadoc (LabTest.java:676, Radiology.java:688) cites 6021/6154 as the gate source.
